questions.question: What are private class fields in JavaScript (ES2022)?
options: Fields declared with the private keyword
Fields prefixed with # that are only accessible within the class body
Fields declared inside the constructor only
Fields that cannot be read, only written
questions.answer: Fields prefixed with # that are only accessible within the class body
questions.explanation: Private class fields use the # prefix (e.g., #name) and are truly private — accessing them outside the class throws a SyntaxError.

questions.question: What must be the first statement in a subclass constructor?
options: this.init()
super()
Object.create()
parent.call(this)
questions.explanation: In a class that extends another, super() must be called before any reference to 'this' in the constructor. It initializes the parent class and sets up the prototype chain.

questions.question: What is prototypal inheritance in JavaScript?
options: Objects inherit from class blueprints like in Java
Objects directly inherit from other objects through a prototype chain
Inheritance is achieved by copying methods between objects
JavaScript does not support inheritance
questions.answer: Objects directly inherit from other objects through a prototype chain
questions.explanation: Every JavaScript object has a [[Prototype]] link to another object. Property lookup traverses this chain until the property is found or null is reached.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of factory functions over classes?
options: Factory functions are faster
Factory functions support true private state via closures without special syntax
Factory functions support inheritance
Factory functions are part of the ES6 standard
questions.answer: Factory functions support true private state via closures without special syntax
questions.explanation: Variables declared inside a factory function are captured by the closure and are truly private, with no way to access them from outside. This is simpler and more flexible before ES2022 private fields.

questions.question: What is a JavaScript mixin?
options: A class that inherits from multiple parent classes
A function that adds reusable methods to a class without classical inheritance
A way to merge two objects into one
An ES6 feature that replaces prototype chains
questions.answer: A function that adds reusable methods to a class without classical inheritance
questions.explanation: Mixins are functions that take a Base class and return a new class with added methods: const Serializable = (Base) => class extends Base { toJSON() {...} }. This enables composition of behaviors.
